workflow.name: Triage LinkedIn notifications and InMails using Gmail, OpenAI, Notion, and Slack
workflow.description:
# LinkedIn Inbox Triage (Gmail Label to Notion + Slack)

This n8n template demonstrates how to use AI to triage LinkedIn emails in your Gmail inbox, so you only see the messages worth your time.  
It filters out automated noise, scores sales likelihood, drafts quick replies for real conversations, stores everything in Notion, and sends you a Slack DM for items you should answer quickly.

## Good to know
* This workflow sends email content to an LLM. Do not use it with sensitive mailboxes unless you are comfortable with that.
* Cost depends on your model choice and token usage. The body is currently limited to 4000 characters to control spend.
* If you want a shorter run window, adjust the `receivedAfter` filter.

## How it works
* Runs on a daily schedule.
* Pulls emails from Gmail using a label you define (example: `LinkedIn`).
* Applies two filters:
  * Keeps only invitations and messages
  * Removes common automated notifications
* Fetches the full email body for better classification.
* Sends the message to an AI agent that returns strict structured JSON:
  * `action` (`reply_quick`, `review`, `ignore`, `block`)
  * `relevancy_score` (0 to 100)
  * `sales_likelihood` (0 to 1)
  * `summary`
  * optional `reply_draft`
* Applies a quality gate to keep high signal messages.
* Writes the output to a Notion database as a ticket.
* Sends a Slack DM only for items marked `reply_quick`.

## How to use
* Create a Gmail label that captures LinkedIn emails, then add the label id to the Gmail node.
* Create a Notion database with fields matching the Notion node mapping.
* Connect your OpenAI, Gmail, Notion, and Slack credentials in n8n.
* Run once manually to verify mapping, then enable the workflow.

## Requirements
* Gmail account
* OpenAI API credentials (or compatible model node)
* Notion database
* Slack account

## Customising this workflow
* Make it more aggressive by increasing the sales threshold or raising the relevancy cutoff.
* Add more filter phrases for your own LinkedIn email language.
* Swap Slack DM for a channel post, or send a daily digest instead of per message.
* Add a redaction step before the AI node if you want to remove signatures or quoted replies.

nodes.name: Pull Messages From with LinkedIn tags
First Filter for non direct messages or invites
Second Filter for non direct messages or invites

options.systemMessage:
You are triaging my LinkedIn related emails from the last 24 hours.

Goal:
- Surface messages worth my time
- Filter out sales and spam aggressively
- Suggest short replies when it looks like an actual person message or InMail content

Rules:
- If it smells like outbound sales, set sales_likelihood high and action to ignore or block.
- If it could be real business, action is reply_quick and include 1 to 3 suggested replies.
- If it is interesting but not urgent, action is review.
- If it is annoying, repetitive, irrelevant, or clearly automated, ignore.
- Block only if it is clearly spammy, pushy sales, or deceptive.

Return ONLY valid JSON matching the schema.

If unsure, choose action ignore, sales_likelihood 0.5, relevancy_score 20, summary minimal

nodes.name: Filter out Irrelevant and Sales heavy requests

parameters.content:
## LinkedIn notifications and InMails triage

This workflow runs daily and turns LinkedIn email noise into a short list of messages worth your attention. It pulls emails from Gmail using a label, filters out common automated notifications, fetches the full email body, and uses an AI agent to classify each message. The result is stored in Notion so you have a searchable inbox, and the most relevant items can trigger a Slack DM so you can reply quickly.

## How it works
* Trigger daily
* Read Gmail messages from a LinkedIn label (last 24h)
* Filter out invitations and automated notifications
* Fetch full email body (not just the snippet)
* AI triage returns structured fields like action, relevancy, and a reply draft
* Save the result to Notion
* Optionally notify you in Slack for items marked reply_quick

## Setup steps
* Create a Gmail label for LinkedIn emails and add the label id in the Gmail node
* Connect Gmail, OpenAI, Notion, and Slack credentials
* Replace the Notion database id and ensure properties match the mapped fields
* Replace the Slack user id (or change to a channel)
* Run once, check a few results, then activate the workflow

## Gmail intake and noise filters
* Pull messages from a Gmail label
* Keep only real LinkedIn messages and InMails
* Remove common automated notifications
Tip: update filter phrases to match your LinkedIn email language

## AI triage
* Sends subject, sender, date, and a body excerpt to the model
* Returns structured output for stable mapping
* Produces action, relevancy, sales likelihood, summary, and optional reply draft

## Privacy note
This workflow sends email content to an LLM.

Do not run this on sensitive mailboxes unless you are comfortable with that.

If needed:
* reduce body length
* redact content before the agent
* store only summaries

## Outputs
* Notion stores every triaged message for later review
* Slack notifies you only for reply_quick items

## Cost and reliability
Notes:
* `maxTokens` is high, which can increase cost
* If parsing fails, reduce body length or simplify the schema
* Consider a fallback:
  * if output invalid, set `action = review`

user.name: Paul Karrmann
user.bio:
I am an HR practitioner and builder who has spent years working at the intersection of HR, systems, and automation. Over time, I grew increasingly frustrated with how AI in HR is discussed: big promises, abstract strategies, and very little that actually helps teams day to day.

This work exists because HR teams deserve better than hype. They deserve tools, workflows, and mental models that make their work easier without turning them into engineers or forcing them into fragile experiments.

data.details: The Filter node can be used to filter items based on a condition. If the condition is met, the item will be passed on to the next node. If the condition is not met, the item will be omitted. Conditions can be combined together by AND(meet all conditions), or OR(meet at least one condition).
